coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots derived from leaves with low toxicity via damaging cytomembrane for broad-spectrum antibacterial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yurong
Zhang, Mengling
Wang, Huibo
Wang, Bo
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection is one of the most serious health hazards for people all over the world. Simultaneously, with the wide application of antibiotics, drug-resistance has become a crucial issue. Numerous nanomaterials have been studied to fight bacteria. Yet few studies point out the relationship between carbon dots (CDs) from leaves and bacteria. In this work, three kinds of N-doped CDs by one-step hydrothermal method were synthesized from osmanthus leaves, tea leaves and milk vetch, respectively, and presented different broad-spectrum antibacterial activities via stemming from the damage of the cytomembrane. Among them, at maximum inhibitory concentration of 1000 μg/mL, CDs from osmanthus leaves (OCDs) show the best antibacterial activity and only 13.09% of the E. coli and 3.37% S. aureus were alive. It is worth noting that so high concentration of OCDs owns low toxicity to cells. In contrast, CDs from milk vetch (MCDs) exhibit the worst antibacterial activity and even 70% of both species survived at the same concentration. This difference of antibacterial activity among the three CDs should put down to their diverse natural-occurring substance. This work provides a new perspective on the selectivity of low-toxicity and high-concentration carbon-based nanomaterials for broad-spectrum antibacterial materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Leaves
Natural substances
Antimicrobial activities
Broad-spectrum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program ( 2018YFE0306105 ), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51972216 , 52041202 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190041 , BK20190828 ), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province ( 2019B010933001 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) , and the 111 Project. ‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of OCDs (HRTEM image as inset). (b) Diameter distribution histogram of OCDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of OCDs, TCDs and MCDs (red, pink and blue lines, respectively). (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of OCDs, TCDs and MCDs (red, pink and blue lines, respectively) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum of OCDs, (b) high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra, (c) N 1s XPS spectra and (d) O 1s XPS spectra of OCDs.
Normalized fluorescence intensity of OCDs influenced by different pH values, ionic strengths in NaCl aqueous solution, stabilization time and temperature.
Absorbance-based viability assay of 293T cells cultured with a series of known concentrations of OCDs for 24 h.

The typical graphs of E. coli and S. aureus with different concentrations of CDs treatment for 24 h and alive ratio of E. coli and S. aureus under different concentrations using UV–Vis spectroscopy method of OCDs, TCDs and MCDs from top to bottom.
(a-b) Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of E. coli and S. aureus with OCDs treatment for 1 h and 37 °C.
The SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus after incubation without and with OCDs at 600 μg/mL for 4 h.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots derived from leaves with low toxicity via damaging cytomembrane for broad-spectrum antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou, 215123 Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: OCDs can effectively inhibit the growth of both E. coli and S. aureus via damaging cytomembrane of cell.

Bacterial infection is one of the most serious health hazards for people all over the world. Simultaneously, with the wide application of antibiotics, drug-resistance has become a crucial issue. Numerous nanomaterials have been studied to fight bacteria. Yet few studies point out the relationship between carbon dots (CDs) from leaves and bacteria. In this work, three kinds of N-doped CDs by one-step hydrothermal method were synthesized from osmanthus leaves, tea leaves and milk vetch, respectively, and presented different broad-spectrum antibacterial activities via stemming from the damage of the cytomembrane. Among them, at maximum inhibitory concentration of 1000 μg/mL, CDs from osmanthus leaves (OCDs) show the best antibacterial activity and only 13.09% of the E. coli and 3.37% S. aureus were alive. It is worth noting that so high concentration of OCDs owns low toxicity to cells. In contrast, CDs from milk vetch (MCDs) exhibit the worst antibacterial activity and even 70% of both species survived at the same concentration. This difference of antibacterial activity among the three CDs should put down to their diverse natural-occurring substance. This work provides a new perspective on the selectivity of low-toxicity and high-concentration carbon-based nanomaterials for broad-spectrum antibacterial materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is one of the most serious hazards for people all over the world and a wide array of antibacterial medications have been widely used to fight it. However, microbial resistance to these drugs has developed on a large scale and greatly reduced the availability of antimicrobials over time, which is becoming an increasingly serious issue [1]. In addition, the number of antibiotics approved in the past decade has continued to decrease significantly [2], which has led to an urgent need for new antimicrobials and therapeutic strategies [3]. There are many methods to be actively pursued to study antibacterial effects today, including finding new antimicrobials from natural products, modifying existing antibiotic and developing antimicrobial peptide [4]. Nanoparticles (NPs) offer a variety of uses for therapeutic applications due to their unique physical properties [5–7]. For example, smaller NPs can enter bacteria and affect bacterial growth [8,9]. In addition, NPs can incorporate rich functional ligands to enhance interaction with bacteria.

Carbon dots (CDs) become a new kind of carbon material accidentally discovered during the process of preparing carbon nanotubes by Xu et al. [10]. CDs possess large specific surface area, easy synthesis, high photostability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and good water solubility [11] and can be widely used in bio-imaging, drug delivery, photo-dynamics, therapy, photovoltaic devices, catalysis, sensing and selective or broad-spectrum antibacterial activities due to their properties [12–20]. Additionally, CDs derived from leaves have been applied to biology, such as detection [21], bio-imaging [22] and patterning agents [23]. But it’s more likely that some plants contain natural substances that have antibacterial properties [24]. However, when they are used as raw materials to synthesize CDs, the change of antibacterial activities is worthy of study and discussion.

In this paper, the CDs derived from osmanthus leaves (OCDs), tea leaves (TCDs) and milk vetch (MCDs) respectively were fabricated via one-step hydrothermal method. In the antibacterial activity test, the OCDs with high concentration of 1000 μg/mL can effectively inhibit the growth of both S. aureus (Gram-positive bacterial) and E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) to 13.09% and 3.37%, respectively, revealing that CDs derived from leaves have broad-spectrum antibacterial activity [25–27]. It is worth mentioning that the OCDs almost reveal no toxicity to cell with so high concentration (1000 μg/mL). While for TCDs, about 40% E. coli and 10% S. aureus are still alive. Besides, even 70% bacteria survive with the presence of MCDs. Further experimental results demonstrate the broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of three CDs should ascribe to damage the cytomembrane of bacteria. Furthermore, the difference of broad-spectrum antibacterial activities in three CDs is mainly due to the various natural-occurring substance in the leaves.
All reagents were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (Beijing, China) and used without further treatment. The 293T cells were acquired from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Science.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) analysis were obtained by a FEI/Philips Tecnai G2 F20 TWIN TEM. A Bruker Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Hyperion) was used to obtain the FTIR spectrum. The UV–Vis absorption measurement was collected by utilizing a Perkin Elmer UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750), while a Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) luminescence spectrometer was adopted for obtaining PL spectra. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured using a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The dynamic light scattering measurement was acquired by using a ZEN3690 zetasizer (Malvern, U.K.). The Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were obtained with a laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope (Leica, TCS-SP5). The SEM images were analysed by Zeiss Supra 55 SEM.

The osmanthus leaves, tea leaves and milk vetch were selected to synthesize the N-doped CDs, which were correspondingly named as OCDs, TCDs and MCDs. Firstly, the fresh leaves were collected, washed, lyophilized and ground to powder. Subsequently, 4 g leaf powder was dispersed in 20 mL of water and transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then the solution was heated at 200 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained yellow solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was taken and passed through 0.22 μm filter to get rid of larger particles. Finally, the obtained three kinds of CDs were stored under 4 °C for further use.

By shaking at 180 rpm in lysogeny broth (LB) medium at 37 °C, E. coli and S. aureus were cultured respectively. The CDs solution with known concentrations was added to co-cultured over time and then optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was measured. Coating the bacteria solution obtained on concretionary LB medium and culturing it at 37 °C aim to get bacterial coating plate.

The CDs solution was diluted in a series of different concentrations from 0 to 1000 μg/mL with 200 μg/mL as the interval, added to the bacterial solution, and shaken at 37 °C under 180 rpm for 4 h in a shaker. Finally, the absorbance of the bacterial solution at the wavelength of 600 nm was measured, and the bacterial viability was calculated on the basis of the following formula,
B a c t e r i a l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
O s
/
O c
× 100 %
where Os and Oc are the values of OD600 of the bacterial solution with different concentrations of CDs and the bacterial solution without CDs, respectively.

The 293T cell lines were cultured in the standard medium at 37 °C in 5% CO2. The cells were incubated in a 96-well plate for 24 h before different concentrations of CDs were added to the cells to test the cytotoxicity of CDs. After 24 h, the relative viabilities of the cells were measured by using colorimetric MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assays. The cells were lysed with acidulated sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). The absorbance at 570 nm was measured by a microplate reader (Bio-Rad 680, USA). Three tests at least were conducted to prove the credibility of the experiment.

The morphology of bacteria was obtained by SEM. Firstly the bacteria solution (with and without CDs treatment) was dropped onto a silicon wafer. Then the bacteria was treated with 2% glutaraldehyde to fix form for 3 h. Thirdly they were dehydrated by ethanol solutions (50, 70, 90, 95, and 100%). After drying, the silicon wafer was sprayed ultrathin gold film by sputtering and imaged with SEM (JSM-7600F, JEOL).

Fig. 1 a displays the TEM image of the obtained OCDs, demonstrating that OCDs are well dispersed in nanoscale. The crystal lattice spacing shown by HRTEM inset in Fig. 1a is displayed as 0.21 nm, revealing the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [28]. Fig. 1b shows the size distribution of OCDs, indicating the diameter is mainly in the range of 4–9 nm. According to the TEM images and size distributions in Fig. S1, the sizes of TCDs and MCDs are mainly in the range of 3–7 nm. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of three CDs were carried out under ZEN3690 zetasizer and the values are close to −20 mV.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of three CDs are exhibited in Fig. 1c. In the FT-IR spectrum of OCDs (pink line), the broad adsorption peak from 3700 to 3030 cm−1 can be thought as the stretching vibrations of OH and NH group. The peak at 1641 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of CO group. The adsorption peaks of the stretching vibrations of CN and CO groups can be observed at 1402 cm−1 and 1095 cm−1, respectively. These hydrophilic functional groups lead to the excellent water solubility of the obtained CDs. The FT-IR spectra of TCDs (red line) and MCDs (blue line) show the similar peak positions, which reveals that they possess the same surface functional groups, including amidogen, carbonyl, ether group and hydroxyl.

Fig. 1d displays the UV–Vis adsorption spectra of OCDs, TCDs and MCDs. In the UV–Vis spectrum, the adsorption peak at 260 nm represents the n-π* transition of amine functional group for OCDs (pink line). While for TCDs (red line) and MCDs (blue line), beside the peak at 260 nm, there is an adsorption peak at 220 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of carbonyl functional group. In addition, all CDs show that the PL spectra is dependent with the excitation wavelength under the excitation wavelength from 410 nm to 490 nm (Fig. S2). The resulting fluorescence intensity gradually red shifted due to defect luminescence [11] and the photoluminescence (PL) has the strongest emission wavelength at 530 nm with the 450 nm excitation.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was done to analyze the structure and chemical components of OCDs. Fig. 2 shows the XPS survey scan spectrum of OCDs, indicating that there are three main elements – C, N and O in the OCDs and the content ratios of these elements are 60%, 9% and 31%. Table S1 shows the content ratios of three CDs. The high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra are respectively presented. In the C 1s spectrum, there peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 287.8 eV tally with sp2 C, CO/CN and CO/CN bonding separately. The spectrum of N 1s can be divided into two peaks. One located at 399.8 eV reveals CN and the other one located at 401.4 eV represents the CN bonding. In the high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra, two peaks at 532.2 eV and 533.0 eV can be ascribed to CO and CO bonding. Compared to the OCDs, the others almost have the same adsorption peaks, demonstrating that they have the same functional groups (Fig. S3 and S4). Generally speaking, the acquired CDs have graphite structure cores containing abundant functional groups on their surfaces.

The fluorescence stability of OCDs was further measured under the effects of different pH value, ionic strength in NaCl aqueous solution, stabilization time and temperature. Firstly, the change of pH value (from strongly acidic condition to alkaline condition) hardly influences the PL intensity of OCDs. Fig. 3 also shows that the PL intensity (excitation wavelength is 450 nm) of OCDs can be hardly affected by the different ionic strength in NaCl aqueous solution, revealing that OCDs are stable in different concentrations of NaCl aqueous solution. Thirdly, we can see that there is no obvious influence on PL intensity even over 6 h in air. Clearly, the PL intensity of OCDs is pH, ionic strength and time independent. At last, an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of OCDs and temperature is presented with the linear regression equation being normalized intensity = 1.61-0.015x (R 2 = 0.998), where x is the value of temperature. Therefore, the PL intensity of OCDs changes with the temperature. The temperature gradually rises from 40 to 80 degrees Celsius, and the PL intensity gradually decreases to about 40%.

Since OCDs are required as an antibacterial agent, the cytotoxicity of the as-synthesized OCDs is particularly important for the following biological applications. So, the 293T cells cultured with different concentrations of OCDs were incubated in 96-well plates for 24 h and the proportion of cells that survived after incubation with OCDs for 24 h is shown in Fig. 4 . When the concentrations of OCDs are at 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 μg/mL, the cell viabilities are 98.5% and 91.2%, 90.2%, 85.7% and 80.1%, respectively. It is apparent that even at high concentration (1000 μg/mL), over 80% of the cells survive, which suggests that the CDs derived from osmanthus leaves have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, meaning it is suitable for further use in biological applications.

The antibacterial activities of CDs on representative bacteria were studied in the following experiments. As shown in Fig. 5 , no matter it is E. coli or S. aureus, their growth is related to the concentrations of CDs. From the left, as the concentration of CDs increases from 0 to 800 μg mL−1 with 200 μg mL−1 as an interval, the survival number of two bacterial survived gradually decreases from the left dat. Among them, OCDs show the best inhibitory effect on both kinds of bacteria, while the TCDs is inferior and MCDs is the worst.

Simultaneously, the alive ratio of both bacteria is calculated by measuring the OD600 value and then divided by the OD600 value of control group are presented in the right side of Fig. 5. As shown, the histograms show that with the increasing concentrations of CDs, the growth of both of E. coli and S. aureus is inhibited. For E. coli with OCDs, the activity ratio decreases to 77.02%, 64.77%, 50.46%, 29.44% and 13.09% when the concentration increases to the 200 μg/mL, 400 μg/mL, 600 μg/mL, 800 μg/mL and 1000 μg/mL, respectively. While for S. aureus with OCDs, at the same concentration, each of the alive ratio is 65.8%, 56.9%, 36.4%, 13.31% and 3.37%. For TCDs, the alive ratio is about 38% for E. coli and 10% for S. aureus. However, 70% of both bacteria are still alive at the highest concentration of MCDs. When the concentration reached to the maximum concentration, the alive ratio had minimal value. To sum up, the activity ratio of both strains decreases continuously with rise in concentration of CDs. At 1000 μg/mL, CDs could almost achieve the complete inhibition of S. aureus and significant reduction in the number of E. coli with OCDs treated. They all show that CDs have no bacterial selectivity and can inhibit the growth of both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Although the effects have been mixed or worse, we speculate that the reason for this difference of antimicrobial activity is that they contain different natural substances.

The obtained results exhibit CDs can effectively inhibit the growth of bacteria at high-concentration 1000 μg/mL. Even at so high concentration, CDs is barely toxic to the 293T cells. Two factors can be speculated as follows: the first one is the different structures of 293T cells and bacteria. The other one is the difference in size. Bacteria are more accessible than cells under the same condition.

To study the mechanism of the bacterial inhibition with CDs treatment, the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs was investigated. S. aureus and E. coli were cultured with CDs at sublethal concentration (600 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 1 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 (a,b), the fluorescence images are intuitively seen when exciting at 405 nm after culturing. The other two identical results are presented in Fig. S5. These similar results prove that CDs are effectively internalized into bacteria. At the same time, the CDs can mainly get into the bacteria by diffusion.

Fig. 7 shows the E. coli and S. aureus were incubated without and with OCDs at 37 °C for 4 h at sublethal dose (400 μg/mL) respectively, and then the morphology of the bacteria was observed under scanning electron microscope (SEM) after dehydration of sample. The SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus without OCDs (control group) respectively indicated that the bacterial surface has obvious and coherent membrane structure. E. coli and S. aureus incubated with OCDs presented that the bacterial cells were roughened and shrunk. The same morphology and results of bacteria treated with TCDs and MCDs are appeared in Fig. S6. There may be damage of the cell wall, which reveals that CDs can destroy the bacterial cell wall. In addition, the outer surface of cells will be coated by CDs, leading to indirect toxicity to cause that bacterial cells stop proliferating [29–32].

The above experimental results indicate that CDs can be used for antibacterial applications and the mechanism of the antibacterial behaviors involves the destruction of cytomembrane. CDs are not only effectively internalized into bacteria, but also can spread into bacteria by diffusion [33,34]. When CDs get inside the bacteria, they will accumulate in DNA/RNA and bind to DNA/RNA, then they affect the second conformation of DNA [24,35,36]. In other words, CDs can loosen the DNA structure and cause the DNA double helix separate. Meanwhile, CDs can form a coating on the surface of bacteria destroy the cell walls of bacteria. Finally, a series of effects cause the death of bacteria and achieve antimicrobial effect.

In this work, three kinds of CDs from different leaves were obtained by one-step hydrothermal method and named as OCDs, TCDs, and MCDs, respectively. OCDs can inhibit the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The inhibitory effect of OCDs on E. coli increases with the increasing concentration of OCDs, achieving the best inhibitory effect at 1000 μg/m L with alive ratio of 13.09%. While for S. aureus, the bacterial activity ratio of OCDs is as low as about 3% at the same concentration of 1000 μg/mL. The other two CDs also show the influence on bacteria, but not as well. The possible reason is that they possess different natural substances. As the concentration of CDs increases, the proportion of activity continues to decrease. Based on the damage of cytomembrane of bacteria, the bacteria shrunk and died. It is the first time to link different leaves to antimicrobial activity, indicating that it may provide a new idea for further searching and screening new materials from natural substances for antibacterial researches.
I (Hui Huang) have made substantial contributions to conception and design of this work.
Yurong Ma has done the experiments and drafted the Figures.
Mengling Zhang, Huibo Wang and Bo Wang have done the tests for this work.
Profs. Yang Liu and Zhenhui Kang provided fund support.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041, BK20190828), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project.‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2020.101222.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Trends in antimicrobial drug development: implications for the future

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supported carbon dots serve as high-performance adsorbent for the retention of trace cadmium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. K. Li, T. Yang, M. L. Chen, J. H. Wang, Supported carbon dots serve as high-performance adsorbent for the retention of trace cadmium, Talanta 180 (2018) 18–24. doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.12.020.
